content.text: Integration vs E2E Tests for AI Workflows

Integration tests and end-to-end (E2E) tests both make real Claude API calls, but they test different things at different levels of the system. Conflating them leads to either an under-tested system (relying only on E2E tests, which are slow and brittle) or an over-expensive one (running integration tests on every PR against the full deployed stack). Understanding where each type of test starts, where it stops, and what it asserts is the first step to building a useful test suite.

The boundary of a test determines what it can detect and what it cannot. An integration test that starts at the Python function level can catch serialisation bugs and API contract changes; it cannot catch routing bugs that only appear when a real HTTP request traverses an nginx reverse proxy. An E2E test can catch the latter but will never give you precise enough feedback to fix a JSON field name mismatch.

This sub-subtopic defines the two test types precisely, shows what assertions are appropriate at each level for AI workflows, provides implementation patterns for both, and covers cost control strategies for keeping a mixed integration + E2E suite affordable.
Boundaries and Scope

attrs.alt: Integration vs E2E test scope: integration covers handler+code+Claude API; E2E adds browser and deployed backend

content.text: Dimension
Integration Test
E2E Test
Entry point
Python function or HTTP endpoint called directly in-process
Browser (Playwright/Selenium) or HTTP client hitting the deployed URL
Infrastructure
Local process, real Anthropic API, no real DB needed if mocked
Fully deployed application: frontend, backend, real DB, real Anthropic API
Claude calls
Real API call through real integration code
Real API call, but through the full deployed stack
Assertion target
JSON response structure, HTTP status, token counts
UI state (element visible, text rendered), HTTP status only
What it catches
API contract changes, serialisation bugs, token budget violations
Deployment misconfiguration, nginx routing errors, cross-origin failures
What it misses
Infrastructure routing, browser rendering, cross-service auth
Fine-grained response structure, token counts, retry behaviour
Typical count
5–20 per Claude integration
1–3 per critical user flow
Run frequency
On merge to main
On deploy to staging and production
Writing Integration Tests for AI Workflows

An integration test for a Claude-powered endpoint verifies the structural contract between the integration layer and the model. It does not assert exact output text — that is the eval suite's job. It asserts that the output meets the structural requirements that the rest of the system depends on:

# tests/integration/test_summary_integration.py
"""
Integration tests for the summary agent endpoint.
These call the real Anthropic API. Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY before running.
Run with: pytest tests/integration/ -m integration
"""
import json, os, time
import pytest, anthropic
from src.summary_agent import summarise_document

pytestmark = pytest.mark.integration

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client():
    return anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

def test_returns_dict_with_bullets_key(client):
    """The summary response must contain a 'bullets' key (list of strings)."""
    result = summarise_document(
        client,
        "The quarterly revenue increased by 12% year-over-year. Customer retention improved "
        "to 94%. Operating costs declined due to infrastructure optimisations.",
    )
    assert isinstance(result, dict), f"Expected dict, got {type(result)}"
    assert "bullets" in result, f"Missing 'bullets' key. Got: {list(result.keys())}"
    assert isinstance(result["bullets"], list), "'bullets' must be a list"
    assert len(result["bullets"]) >= 1, "'bullets' list must not be empty"

def test_output_token_budget(client):
    """Summary must not exceed 300 output tokens (cost control)."""
    result = summarise_document(
        client,
        "A short document about nothing in particular.",
    )
    # The function must attach usage metadata for budget verification
    assert "_output_tokens" in result, "Function must return _output_tokens for budget tracking"
    assert result["_output_tokens"] <= 300, (
        f"Output token budget exceeded: {result['_output_tokens']} > 300"
    )

def test_handles_very_short_document(client):
    """A one-sentence document should not cause an exception."""
    result = summarise_document(client, "Hello.")
    assert isinstance(result, dict)

def test_handles_very_long_document(client):
    """A document near the context limit should not cause an exception."""
    long_text = "This is a test sentence. " * 500
    result = summarise_document(client, long_text)
    assert isinstance(result, dict)
    assert "bullets" in result

def test_latency_under_10_seconds(client):
    """A summary call must complete within 10 seconds (p99 SLO proxy)."""
    start = time.monotonic()
    summarise_document(client, "A short document to measure latency.")
    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    assert elapsed < 10.0, f"Latency too high: {elapsed:.1f}s"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    pytest.main([__file__, "-v"])
Writing E2E Tests for AI Workflows

An E2E test for a Claude-powered feature exercises the full deployed stack through its external interface. Because the model's output is non-deterministic, E2E tests assert UI-level structural properties — the response container is visible, no error message appears — not the text of the response:

# tests/e2e/test_summary_e2e.py
"""
E2E tests for the summary feature using Playwright.
Run against the staging environment:
    BASE_URL=https://staging.example.com pytest tests/e2e/ -m e2e
Requires Playwright: pip install playwright && playwright install chromium
"""
import os, re
import pytest
from playwright.sync_api import Page, expect

pytestmark = pytest.mark.e2e
BASE_URL = os.environ.get("BASE_URL", "http://localhost:5173")

@pytest.fixture
def page(playwright):
    browser = playwright.chromium.launch()
    context = browser.new_context()
    page    = context.new_page()
    yield page
    browser.close()

def test_summary_result_appears(page: Page):
    """
    The summary result container must appear after submitting a document.
    We do not assert the summary text — only that the container is visible
    and does not show an error state.
    """
    page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")
    page.fill("[data-testid=document-input]", "Revenue grew 12% year-over-year.")
    page.click("[data-testid=submit-button]")
    # Wait for the result container — up to 30s for the API call
    result_container = page.locator("[data-testid=summary-result]")
    expect(result_container).to_be_visible(timeout=30_000)

def test_no_error_message_on_valid_input(page: Page):
    """
    A valid input must not show an error banner.
    """
    page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")
    page.fill("[data-testid=document-input]", "A valid document text.")
    page.click("[data-testid=submit-button]")
    # Check that no error banner appeared within 30s
    error_banner = page.locator("[data-testid=error-banner]")
    expect(error_banner).not_to_be_visible(timeout=30_000)

def test_loading_state_shown_during_call(page: Page):
    """
    A loading indicator must appear between submit and result.
    This verifies that the UI handles the async API call correctly.
    """
    page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")
    page.fill("[data-testid=document-input]", "Some document text.")
    page.click("[data-testid=submit-button]")
    # Loading spinner should appear immediately after click
    spinner = page.locator("[data-testid=loading-spinner]")
    expect(spinner).to_be_visible(timeout=3_000)
    # And disappear when the result arrives
    expect(spinner).not_to_be_visible(timeout=30_000)
Cost Control Strategies
Both integration tests and E2E tests consume API credits. Without cost controls, a CI suite that runs on every commit can accumulate significant monthly costs:
Strategy
How to Apply
Typical Saving
Run on merge to main only
Use `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` in the CI job condition
Eliminates all PR-level API spend for integration tests
Use a fast low-cost model in test fixtures
Override the model to claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 in integration test fixtures, not the production model
3–10× cost reduction per test call
Set low max_tokens in test calls
Integration tests do not need long responses — set max_tokens=150 in the test fixture
Proportional reduction in output token cost
Gate evals on file-path changes
Only run eval jobs when prompts/, src/, or tools/ change
Skips eval runs on docs, config, and test-only PRs
Limit integration test count
If you have more than 20 integration tests, audit for cases that could be layer-2 unit tests with mocked Claude
Each mocked test is free; reduces the integration suite size
Deciding Which Type to Write
A simple question determines which test type to write for a given scenario:
Does the bug require a deployed application to reproduce?
If yes, it is an E2E test. Deployment bugs (missing env vars, misconfigured nginx, CORS issues) can only be caught when the full stack is running.
Does the bug require a real Claude API call to reproduce?
If yes but no deployed stack is needed, it is an integration test. Serialisation bugs, token budget overruns, and API contract changes are caught here.
Does the bug appear in the code logic around the API call?
If the bug is in retry logic, routing, or response parsing — not in the model's output — it is a layer-2 unit test with a mocked client. No API call needed.
Is the bug a change in what the model outputs?
Prompt regressions, refusal boundary shifts, and JSON schema violations go in the eval suite. They require real API calls but also require a baseline to compare against.

If you cannot decide between integration and E2E, default to integration. Integration tests are cheaper, faster, give more precise error messages, and can run locally without a deployed environment. E2E tests are valuable but expensive — use them sparingly for the critical paths that must work in production.
Recording and Replaying Integration Test Responses

Integration tests that call the real Anthropic API are slow and cost money. A response recording layer — sometimes called a VCR cassette — captures the real API response on the first run and replays it on subsequent runs. This gives the correctness guarantee of a real API call while running as fast as a unit test for the 99% of runs that do not need a fresh response:

# tests/integration/cassette.py
"""
Simple response cassette for integration tests.
On the first run (RECORD=true), calls the real API and saves the response.
On subsequent runs, loads the saved response and skips the API call.
Set RECORD=true environment variable to refresh a cassette.
"""
import json, os
from pathlib import Path
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

_CASSETTE_DIR = Path("tests/integration/cassettes")

class Cassette:
    def __init__(self, name: str):
        self._path    = _CASSETTE_DIR / f"{name}.json"
        self._record  = os.environ.get("RECORD", "false").lower() == "true"

    def __enter__(self):
        if not self._record and self._path.exists():
            self._start_playback()
        return self

    def __exit__(self, *args):
        if hasattr(self, "_patcher"):
            self._patcher.stop()

    def _start_playback(self) -> None:
        saved = json.loads(self._path.read_text())

        fake_block = MagicMock()
        fake_block.text = saved["text"]
        fake_block.type = "text"
        fake_response   = MagicMock()
        fake_response.content = [fake_block]
        fake_response.usage.output_tokens  = saved["output_tokens"]
        fake_response.usage.input_tokens   = saved["input_tokens"]
        fake_response.stop_reason          = saved["stop_reason"]

        self._patcher = patch(
            "anthropic.resources.messages.Messages.create",
            return_value=fake_response,
        )
        self._patcher.start()

def record_response(cassette_name: str, response) -> None:
    """Call this after a real API response to save it as a cassette."""
    _CASSETTE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    path = _CASSETTE_DIR / f"{cassette_name}.json"
    text = response.content[0].text if response.content else ""
    path.write_text(json.dumps({
        "text":          text,
        "output_tokens": response.usage.output_tokens,
        "input_tokens":  response.usage.input_tokens,
        "stop_reason":   response.stop_reason,
    }, indent=2))

# tests/integration/test_summary_cassette.py
"""
Integration tests using cassettes.
First run: RECORD=true pytest tests/integration/test_summary_cassette.py
Subsequent runs: pytest tests/integration/test_summary_cassette.py (no API call)
"""
import os
import pytest
import anthropic
from tests.integration.cassette import Cassette, record_response
from src.summary_agent import summarise_document

pytestmark = pytest.mark.integration

def test_billing_summary_structure():
    """
    Assert that the summary response has the required structure.
    First run records the API response; subsequent runs replay it.
    """
    with Cassette("billing-document-summary"):
        client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "test"))
        result = summarise_document(
            client,
            "Revenue increased 12% year-over-year. Customer churn dropped to 6%.",
        )
        assert isinstance(result, dict)
        assert "bullets" in result
        assert isinstance(result["bullets"], list)

def test_technical_summary_has_at_least_one_bullet():
    """A technical document must produce at least one bullet point."""
    with Cassette("technical-document-summary"):
        client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "test"))
        result = summarise_document(
            client,
            "The service experienced elevated p99 latency on 2025-06-15 due to a misconfigured "
            "connection pool. The pool size was reduced from 200 to 50, which resolved the issue.",
        )
        assert len(result.get("bullets", [])) >= 1
Parallelising Integration Tests

Integration tests are I/O-bound — most of the wall-clock time is waiting for the Anthropic API to respond. Parallelising them across multiple workers cuts the total CI time proportionally. Use `pytest-xdist` with a worker count matching the number of independent tests:

# .github/workflows/integration.yml
name: Integration tests

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt pytest-xdist

      - name: Run integration tests (4 workers in parallel)
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          pytest tests/integration/ -m integration \
            -n 4 \
            --timeout=60 \
            -v

Four workers are a reasonable default for a suite of 10–20 integration tests. Each worker makes independent API calls, so wall-clock time drops to roughly `total_tests / workers × avg_latency`. The Anthropic API rate limits are per-organisation, not per-connection, so parallelising within a moderate worker count is safe. If CI hits rate limit errors, reduce the worker count or add `--rate-limit-pause=2` logic in your fixture.

# tests/integration/conftest.py — rate-limit-aware fixture
import os, time
import pytest
import anthropic

_LAST_CALL_TIME: dict[str, float] = {}
_MIN_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 0.5   # 500ms between calls per worker

@pytest.fixture(scope="function")
def client():
    """
    Client fixture with a minimum inter-call interval.
    When running with -n 4, each worker gets its own client instance.
    The rate limiting here is per-worker, not global — sufficient to
    avoid 429s on small suites but adjust for larger suites.
    """
    worker_id = os.environ.get("PYTEST_XDIST_WORKER", "main")
    last = _LAST_CALL_TIME.get(worker_id, 0.0)
    elapsed = time.monotonic() - last
    if elapsed < _MIN_INTERVAL_SECONDS:
        time.sleep(_MIN_INTERVAL_SECONDS - elapsed)
    _LAST_CALL_TIME[worker_id] = time.monotonic()
    return anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])
Summary

Integration tests for AI workflows start at the function or HTTP endpoint level, make real Claude API calls, and assert structural properties of the response (valid JSON, required keys, token budget, latency). E2E tests start at the browser or deployed HTTP level and assert UI-level structural properties (container visible, no error banner) without asserting content. Integration tests belong on merge-to-main CI; E2E tests belong on deploy-to-staging CI. A cassette recording layer captures real API responses on first run and replays them on subsequent runs, making most CI runs free and instant. Parallelising integration tests with `pytest-xdist` reduces wall-clock time proportionally to the number of workers. Cost is controlled by using a cheap test model in integration fixtures, setting low max_tokens, gating eval runs on relevant file changes, and limiting integration test count by migrating logic-only tests down to the mocked unit layer. When choosing between test types, start with the lowest layer that can catch the bug — integration is cheaper and more precise than E2E for most AI workflow bugs.
